title: Graphs of motion in one dimension

concepts.title: Average Velocity and Instantaneous Velocity  graphs
concepts.conceptBody:
Graphs (Straight Line Motion)

With the help of graphs we visualise the variation of position (x), velocity (v), and acceleration (a) of a moving particle with time. Plotting time $\left( \text{t} \right)$ on $\text{X}$-axis and $\text{x},\text{v}$, a on $\text{Y}$-axis we get three useful graphs:

(i) $\text{x}-\text{t}$ graphs (ii) $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graphs (iii) at graphs

Average Velocity

If a particle passes a point $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}} \right)$ at time $\text{t}=\text{ti}$ and reaches $\text{Q}\left( {{\text{x}}_{\text{f}}} \right)$ at a later time instant $\text{t}={{\text{t}}_{\text{f}}}$, its average velocity in the interval $\text{PQ}$ is ${{\text{V}}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}=\frac{{{\text{x}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}}}{{{\text{t}}_{\text{f}}}-\text{t}}$.

This expression suggests that the average velocity is equal to the slope of the line (chord) joining the points corresponding to $\text{P},\text{Q}$ on the $\text{x}-\text{t}$ graph.

Instantaneous Velocity: 

Consider the motion of the particle between the two points $\text{P}$ and $\text{Q}$ on the $\text{x}-\text{t}$ graph shown. As the point $\text{Q}$ is brought closer and closer to the point P. the time interval between PQ (At, At', At"',.. ) get progressively smaller. The average velocity for each time interval is the slope of the appropriate dotted line (PQ, $\left. \text{P}{{\text{Q}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}},\text{P}{{\text{Q}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}},.. \right)$. As the point $\text{Q}$ approaches $\text{P}$, the time interval approaches zero, but at the same time the slope of the dotted line approaches that of the tangent to the curve at the point $\text{P}$. As $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}\to 0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{\text{av}}}\left( =\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}/\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t} \right).\to {{\text{V}}_{\text{inst }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$

Geometrically, As $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}\to 0$, chord $\text{PQ}\to $ tangent at $\text{P}$.

Hence the instantaneous velocity at $\text{P}$ in the slope of the tangent at $\text{P}$ in the $\text{x}-\text{t}$ graph. When the slope of the $\text{x}-\text{t}$ graph is positive, $\text{v}$ is positive (as at the point $\text{A}$ in figure). At $\text{C},\text{v}$ is negative because the tangent has negative slope. The instantaneous velocity at point $B$ (turning point) is zero as the slope is zero.

concepts.title: Area under v-t Graph
concepts.conceptBody:
Area under v-t Graph

Let us now discuss the problem of determining displacement from a $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph. For motion at constant velocity, the $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph is a horizontal line, as shown in the figure.

Since $\text{vv}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}$, the displacement Ax in a time integral $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$ is given by $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\text{v }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$.

This is just the area of the shaded rectangle of height $\text{v}$ and width $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }t$.

Note that the unit of this area is $\left( \text{m}/\text{s} \right)\left( \text{s} \right)=\text{m}$.

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\text{u }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}=$ height of rectangle $\text{x}$ base = area of rectangle under $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph.

Let us now consider the case when the velocity is not constant. Let the velocity be ${{\text{v}}_{1}}$ for $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{1}}$ seconds, ${{\text{v}}_{2}}$ for $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{2}}$ seconds, ${{\text{v}}_{3}}$ for $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{3}}$ seconds.

Displacement

$=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{3}}$

$={{\text{v}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{1}}+{{\text{v}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{2}}+{{\text{v}}_{3}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{3}}=$sum of areas of the three shaded rectangle.

$\Rightarrow $ Displacement $=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=$ area under $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph.

For the most general case, the $\text{v}$-t graph can be a curve i.e. velocity may change continuously with time as shown. To calculate displacement during an interval $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$, we divide this interval into many small intervals $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{1}},+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{2}},+..+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{\text{n}}} \right)$. If the number of sub-intervals is made very large (i.e. $\text{n}$ goes on increasing), each interval becomes very small $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{\text{i}}}\to 0 \right)$ and displacement during each of these subintervals may be taken as the area of the shaded rectangles as shown. The approximation improves as the number of rectangles (i.e. sub-intervals) is increased.

$\text{displacement }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left. ={{\text{v}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{1}}+{{\text{v}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{2}}+\ldots +{{\text{V}}_{\text{n}}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{\text{n}}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}\to \infty ,\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{\text{i}}}\to 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ displacement }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ total }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ under }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}-\text{t }\!\!~\!\!\text{ curve}\text{.}$

Since a negative velocity causes a negative displacement, areas below the time axis are taken negative.

In a similar way, we can see that $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ v}=\text{a }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$ leads to the conclusion that area under a $-\text{t}$ graph gives the change in velocity Av during that interval.

As the number of sub-intervals is increased $\left( n\to \infty \right)$, the sum of the areas of the rectangles approaches the area under the curve.

concepts.title: Uniformly Accelerated Motion Graph
concepts.conceptBody:
Uniformly Accelerated Motion Graph

Motion with Uniform Velocity u

Consider a particle moving along $\text{X}$-axis with uniform velocity $u$ starting from the point $x={{x}_{i}}$ at $t=0$. Equations of $x,v,a$ are:

$\text{x}\left( \text{t} \right)={{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}}+\text{ut};\text{v}\left( \text{t} \right)=\text{u};\text{a}\left( \text{t} \right)=0$

${{x}^{-}}t$ graph is a straight line of slope $u$ through ${{x}_{i}}$

  • as velocity is constant, $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph is a horizontal line.
  • a-t graph coincides with time axis because a $=0$ at all time instants.

Uniformly Accelerated Motion $\left( a\ne 0 \right)$ 

$\text{x}\left( \text{t} \right)={{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}}+\text{ut}+\frac{1}{2}\text{at}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

$\text{v}\left( \text{t} \right)=\text{u}+\text{at}$

$\text{a}\left( \text{t} \right)=\text{a}$

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\left( \text{t} \right)$ is a quadratic polynomial in terms of $\text{t}$. Hence ${{x}^{-}}t$ graph is a parabola.
  • $\text{v}\left( \text{t} \right)$ is a linear polynomial in terms of t. Hence ${{v}^{-}}t$ graph is a straight line of slope a.
  • a-t graph is a horizontal line because a is constant.

concepts.title: Important points to remember
concepts.conceptBody:
Important Points to Remember 

  • For uniformly accelerated motion $\left( \text{a}\ne 0 \right),\text{x}-\text{t}$ graph is a parabola (opening upwards if a $>0$ and opening downwards if a $<0$ ). The slope of tangent at any point of the parabola gives the velocity at that instant.
  • For uniformly accelerated motion $\left( \text{a}\ne 0 \right),\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph is straight line whose slope gives the acceleration of the particle.
  • In general, the slope of tangent in $\text{x}-\text{t}$ graph is velocity and the slope of tangent in v-t graph is the acceleration.
  • The area under a-t graph gives the change in velocity.
  • The area between the $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph and the time-axis gives the distance traveled by the particle, if we take all areas as positive; shaded area = distance covered in the time interval $t={{t}_{1}}$ to $\text{t}={{\text{t}}_{2}}$. (see example 9)
  • Area under $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph gives displacement if areas below the t-axis are taken negative. (see example 9)
  • Slopes of $\text{v}-\text{t}$ or $\text{s}-\text{t}$ graphs can never be infinite at any point, because infinite slope of $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph means infinite acceleration. Similarly, infinite slope of s-f graph means infinite velocity. Hence, the following graphs are not possible:

          

  • At one time, two values of velocity or displacement are not possible. Hence, the following graphs are not acceptable:

       

  • Different values of displacements in $\text{s}-\text{t}$ graph corresponding to given $\text{v}-\text{t}$ graph can be calculated just by calculating areas under $\text{v}-\text{f}$ graph. There is no need of using equations like $\text{v}=\text{u}+$ at, etc.
